does: This paper addresses machine unlearning in Mixture-of-Experts language models by proposing TRACE, a method that detects and calibrates forget-critical experts. The core observation is that forget data disproportionately activates certain experts while those same experts receive weak activation from retain data, creating a routing mismatch that leaves them under-regularized. TRACE works in two stages: first identifying which experts are critical to forgetting using offline activation statistics, then reweighting token-level retain losses to equalize each selected expert's activation frequency between forget and retain data. Experiments on WMDP and MUSE-BOOKS benchmarks across multiple MoE language models demonstrate consistent improvements in the forget-utility trade-off, achieving approximately 9% relative utility gains while maintaining effective unlearning.
connects: This work extends recent progress in machine unlearning for large language models by tackling the previously underexplored challenge of sparse mixture-of-experts architectures. It builds on prior unlearning methods that use retain-side regularization to preserve model utility while removing forget knowledge, but recognizes that these techniques fail in MoE settings where routing patterns create fundamental asymmetries. The paper connects to broader research on understanding expert activation patterns and routing behavior in MoE models, as well as work on calibration and reweighting strategies in neural networks. It responds to the limitation that existing dense-model unlearning approaches cannot directly transfer to sparse architectures, requiring a new understanding of how forgetting manifests differently across expert allocation.
enables: This work opens several directions for improving unlearning in sparse model architectures. It establishes that routing-aware analysis is essential for effective unlearning in MoE models, potentially motivating similar architectural considerations in other sparse and conditional computation systems. The calibration approach could extend to other tasks requiring selective model modification, such as bias mitigation or knowledge editing in MoE systems. Future work might explore dynamic recalibration during unlearning, investigate how routing patterns change post-unlearning, or develop theoretical guarantees about expert coverage. The method also creates opportunities to study whether similar mismatches occur in other specialized architectures like adapter-based models or retrieval-augmented systems, advancing the general understanding of unlearning beyond dense architectures.
